steps.title: Check if the equations are equivalent
steps.content: The second equation is $15x + 21y = 33$. Divide by 3: $$\frac{15x + 21y}{3} = \frac{33}{3}$$ This simplifies to $5x + 7y = 11$, which is exactly the first equation. Both equations represent the same line—there are infinitely many solutions.

steps.title: Solve for the parametric form of points on the line
steps.content: Define $L(x,y)=5x+7y$. A correct parametrization must make $L(x,y)=11$ for every value of $r$. Set $r=[0,1]$ and evaluate each choice. $\left(r,-\frac{5r}{7}+\frac{11}{7}\right)$ returns $[11,11]$, so it works for the whole line.

steps.title: Confirm the candidate
steps.content: For $x=r$ and $y=-\frac{5r}{7}+\frac{11}{7}$. Substitution gives $5r+7\left(-\frac{5r}{7}+\frac{11}{7}\right)=5r-5r+11=11$, true for every $r$. $\left(r,-\frac{5r}{7}+\frac{11}{7}\right)$ is correct.
